title: The Politics of State Feminism: Innovation in Comparative Research By Dorothy E. McBride and Amy G. Mazur Temple University Press. 2010. 306 pages. $74.50 cloth
abstract: Feminist research in many disciplines is increasingly comparative in ambition, scope and method. Feminist theory and politics aspire to a pragmatic efficacy that privileges causal arguments; the gold standard of causal analysis requires multiple examples, either longitudinal data, strategically chosen or randomly assigned cases with controls, or both. Growing appreciation of globalization as a primary social force in the contemporary world has also driven feminist scholars and activists toward comparison. Additional momentum comes from what might be termed the “intersectional turn” in feminist research. That is, feminist researchers seek to understand how gender, race, class, sexuality, nation and globalization jointly organize social life. Comparisons greatly facilitate intersectional, global and causal analyses. McBride (Professor Emerita of Political Science at Florida Atlantic University) and Mazur (Distinguished Professor of Political Science at Washington State University) are part of an international network of feminist scholars who have spent the past 15 years conceiving and conducting comparative research on gender politics and the state. The Politics of State Feminism is strongest as a synthesis and extension of the insights from the collective comparative efforts of the Research Network on Gender Politics and the State (RNGS).
